title: The Birchbark Canoe: Artifact or Relationship?
sumoView: Canoe -> WaterVehicle -> TransportationDevice -> Device -> Artifact -> Object -> Physical -> Entity. A manufactured object for water transportation, constructed from birchbark panels over a cedar or spruce wood frame, waterproofed with pine pitch. Classified by function and material composition.
indigenousView: A being with spirit, born from the birch tree's gift. The canoe teaches togetherness — paddling in unison requires trust and relationship. Building one is ceremony: the maker offers tobacco to the birch before harvest, gives thanks to the tree for its sacrifice, and understands that the canoe carries the spirit of the forest into the water. The Anishinaabe word for the birchbark canoe encodes this relationship; it is not a tool but a partner. Traditions: Anishinaabe, Algonquin, Abenaki, Cree, Mi'kmaq, Innu, Attikamek.
teachingPoint: Both descriptions are true. They answer different questions: WHAT something is vs. WHAT something means. SUMO classifies by function — the canoe moves people on water. Anishinaabe knowledge weaves relationship, responsibility, and spirit into every object. The canoe is simultaneously a WaterVehicle and a teacher of togetherness. Knowing only the SUMO view, you can build a boat. Knowing only the Indigenous view, you understand why the journey matters. Both together is the fuller picture.

title: Food Preservation: Chemistry or Community Obligation?
sumoView: FoodPreservation -> ChemicalProcess -> InternalChange -> Process. Biochemical transformation preventing microbial growth through heat, salt, acid, dehydration, or fermentation. Governed by temperature thresholds, water activity levels, and pH measurements. Success is measured by shelf life and pathogen elimination.
indigenousView: Keeping food is keeping life. Pemmican-making connects the hunter's gift, the berry picker's knowledge, the elder's recipe wisdom, and the next generation's survival into one act. The process carries obligations: you smoke enough fish for the whole community, not just your family. You share the first jar of canned berries. You teach the process so the knowledge does not die with you. Among the Cree and Métis, pemmican carried entire communities through winter; the obligation to make enough was understood as social law, not choice.
teachingPoint: SUMO sees chemistry; heritage traditions see community obligation. Both are necessary — the chemistry keeps you safe, the obligation keeps you human. The SUMO view tells you HOW to preserve food without killing people (critical safety information). The Indigenous view tells you WHY food preservation matters and WHO it serves. A learner who understands only the chemistry can make pemmican safely but may miss that the recipe encodes social relationships. A learner who understands only the obligation may make unsafe food. The full picture requires both lenses.

title: Caribou Clothing: Manufacturing or Living Knowledge?
sumoView: Clothing -> WearableItem -> Artifact -> Object -> Physical -> Entity. A manufacturing process converting raw animal hide into functional garments through stages: butchering, fleshing, scraping, brain-tanning or smoke-tanning, cutting, and sewing. The finished parka is an Artifact with thermal insulation properties derived from the hollow-shaft structure of caribou hair.
indigenousView: The caribou clothing cycle (~300 hours from hunt to finished parka) embodies Pilimmaksarniq (the concept of developing skills through practice and observation) and Pijitsirniq (the concept of serving the community and the environment). Each stitch carries knowledge passed from mother to daughter across generations. The caribou that gave its life requires that nothing be wasted — every part of the animal serves a purpose. The parka is not insulation; it is the visible evidence of 300 hours of community investment in one person's survival, and the living record of everything the maker's mother and grandmother knew.
teachingPoint: SUMO counts the object; Inuit Qaujimajatuqangit counts the relationships. A parka is not just insulation — it is evidence of 300 hours of community investment in one person's survival. The SUMO view can model the thermal properties of caribou hair and optimize for insulation value. IQ situates the same object in a web of obligation, skill transmission, and reciprocal relationship with the animal. Both are true. The learner who sees only the artifact misses the IQ principles encoded in every seam. The learner who sees only the IQ principles cannot explain why the garment works.

title: The Seasonal Round: Calendar or Conversation?
sumoView: SeasonalRound -> SeasonalCycle -> TimeInterval -> TemporalRegion -> Abstract -> Entity. A recurring period defined by astronomical or meteorological boundaries. Seasons are divisions of the year based on measurable phenomena: solstices, equinoxes, temperature ranges, precipitation patterns. The calendar imposes structure on time by dividing it into equal or fixed units.
indigenousView: The seasonal round is not a calendar — it is a conversation with the land. Each season brings responsibilities, not just weather. The Cree seasonal round has names for times of year that encode ecological knowledge: the time when the geese arrive, the time when the berries are ripe, the time when the ice is safe to walk. The round connects harvesting, ceremony, travel, and rest into a living relationship with place. The seasons are not passed — they are visited. Indigenous communities do not experience the round as time divided; they experience it as relationship deepened.
teachingPoint: SUMO divides time; Indigenous knowledge listens to it. The Western calendar is imposed on the land; the seasonal round emerges from it. This is not a trivial difference. A calendar tells you what month it is; the seasonal round tells you what your obligations are. A community that follows a seasonal round does not ask 'what day is it?' but 'what is the land telling us to do?' The SUMO view enables cross-cultural comparison and temporal reasoning. The Indigenous view enables right relationship with a specific place. Neither is complete without the other.

title: Governance: Organization or Practice?
sumoView: GovernmentOrganization -> Organization -> Group -> Agent -> Entity. A structured entity with decision-making authority, membership criteria, defined roles, and procedural rules. Governance is a noun: there is a government, and it governs. Western governance systems are typically represented as organizational charts with hierarchical authority.
indigenousView: Governance through Aajiiqatigiinniq (consensus decision-making) is not an organization — it is a practice. There is no separate 'government' in the Western sense — governance lives in the relationships between people, expressed through protocols and gatherings. Among the Inuit, a community decision is not made by a leader who votes or rules; it is made when everyone present has been heard and a consensus has emerged through the process of listening. Authority is earned through demonstrated wisdom and community trust, not through office. Governance dissolves and reconstitutes with each decision; it is not a standing institution.
teachingPoint: SUMO needs a noun (organization); Inuit Qaujimajatuqangit governance is a verb (practicing consensus). The tension reveals that not all governance fits in an org chart. The SUMO view enables modeling governance structures as entities with properties. The IQ view reveals that some governance systems exist as practices rather than institutions — they cannot be diagrammed without distorting them. This is a deep epistemological tension: SUMO's foundational categories assume things exist as entities; some Indigenous concepts exist as relationships. Both views are needed: the SUMO view for cross-system interoperability, the IQ view for understanding how governance actually works in practice.
